11: dia tes charitos tou kyriou Iesou pisteuomen sothenai (dia tēs charitos tou kyriou Iēsou pisteuomen sōthēnai): Peter's climactic speech at the Jerusalem council: 'we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we are saved, just as they are'. The kathos kakeinoi ('just as they also') is the decisive reversal — not Gentiles saved like Jews, but Jews saved like Gentiles: by grace through faith, not by Torah-observance. Charis (grace) as the mode, pisteuomen (we believe) as the means, sōthēnai (to be saved) as the end — Pauline soteriology in Petrine formulation.
1: ean mē peritmnēthēte tō ethei tō Mōuseōs ou dynasthe sōthēnai ('unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses you cannot be saved'): the Judaizers' statement ties salvation to Torah-observance via the covenant-entry rite. The ean mē conditional makes circumcision necessary for salvation — not merely beneficial but soteriologically essential. Paul's response in Galatians (3:1-5; 5:2-6) is his extended refutation of exactly this position.
2: staseōs kai zētēseōs ouk oligēs ('no small dispute and debate'): stasis (strife, sedition) and zētēsis (questioning, dispute) together describe a sharp public controversy. The Lukan understatement ouk oligēs (not small = considerable) mirrors Galatians' account of Paul 'withstanding Peter to the face' (Gal 2:11) — the confrontation was serious. The church's decision to send a delegation to Jerusalem acknowledges that this requires authoritative resolution beyond Antioch's capacity.
3: ekdiēgoumenoi tēn epistrophēn tōn ethnōn ('describing the conversion of the Gentiles'): epistrophē (turning, conversion) is the standard conversion-term (the noun form of epistrephō); the Gentiles are 'those who have turned' from idols to God. The reports they gave in Phoenicia and Samaria produce charan megalēn (great joy) in the communities there — the same joy-marker for eschatological arrival. The groundwork for Jerusalem's reception is laid by the journey itself.
4: eis Ierousalēm paredechthēsan apo tēs ekklēsias kai tōn apostolōn kai tōn presbyterōn ('they were welcomed by the church and the apostles and elders'): the triple reception (church + apostles + elders) indicates formal recognition, not just hospitality. The apostles and elders represent the theological and administrative authority; the whole church represents the community whose practice is at stake. Both dimensions — doctrinal and communal — are engaged in the council process.
5: exanestēsan de tines tōn apo tēs haireseōs tōn Pharisaiōn pepisteukotos ('some who belonged to the party of the Pharisees who had believed stood up'): hairesis (party, sect) is not pejorative in Luke's usage — simply a philosophical-religious school. The Pharisees are the Torah-observant wing of the Jerusalem church; their demand (dei: it is necessary) mirrors the Judaizers' Antioch position. Converted Pharisees brought both rigorous hermeneutical skill and intense covenant-loyalty to the church.
6: synēchthēsan te hoi apostoloi kai hoi presbyteroi idein peri tou logou toutou ('the apostles and elders gathered to consider this matter'): the Jerusalem council is an apostolic-presbyteral deliberative body; its structure mirrors the Jewish Sanhedrin's form while its authority derives from the apostolic eyewitness foundation. The verb idein (to see, consider) indicates careful discernment, not a hasty ruling.
7: pollēs de zētēseōs genomenes anastas Petros eipen ('after much discussion Peter stood up and said'): the deliberative process was genuine — much debate (pollēs zētēseōs) before Peter's intervention. Peter appeals to aph' hēmerōn archaīōn (from early days) — the Cornelius event as the foundational precedent, earlier even than the first missionary journey. God's choice of Peter for the Cornelius mission makes him the witness of God's prior action, not the author of a new policy.
8: kardiognōstēs ho theos (God the heart-knower): the same compound title as Acts 1:24, used in the prayer for Matthias's replacement — the God who knows hearts is the decisive witness to Cornelius's genuine faith. edōken to Pneuma to Hagion autois kathōs kai hēmin ('gave the Holy Spirit to them just as also to us'): the Spirit-gift to uncircumcised Gentiles is God's own verdict on their acceptability — an argument from divine action rather than theological deduction.
9: katharisas tē pistei tas kardias autōn ('cleansing their hearts by faith'): the heart-cleansing is the internal work corresponding to the ritual purity that circumcision-advocates were requiring externally. Pistis (faith) is the divine cleansing agent — not Torah-observance, not ritual, but faith. The verb diekrine (he made no distinction) insists on the absolute parity of the cleansing: identical hearts, identical Spirit, identical faith, identical purity.
10: nun oun ti peirazete ton theon ('so now why do you test God?'): testing God is the Exodus idiom for Israel's faithless complaint against God's provision (Exod 17:2; Ps 95:9). zygon (zygon, yoke): the Torah-as-yoke metaphor appears in rabbinic literature as positive ('the yoke of the kingdom of heaven'); Peter deploys it critically — a yoke hēmeis oute hoi pateres hēmōn ischysamen bastasai (neither we nor our fathers were able to bear). Jesus's contrasting easy yoke (Matt 11:29-30) is the implicit antithesis.
12: esigēsen de pan to plēthos ('the whole assembly became silent'): the silence after Peter's speech marks a rhetorical turning point — the question-raising has been addressed. Barnaban kai Paulon exēgoumenon hosa epoiēsen ho theos sēmeia kai terata en tois ethnesin di' autōn: Barnabas precedes Paul here (as in the first missionary journey's early chapters, before Paul's prominence grew); the signs and wonders confirm the mission's divine authorization to the Jerusalem audience.
13: meta de to sigēsai autous apekrithē Iakōbos ('after they fell silent James answered'): James's position as speaker last and as the one who delivers the judgment (krisis, v.19) confirms his leadership role in the Jerusalem church by Acts 15. As the Lord's brother, his authority is not apostolic in the Twelve-sense but familial and earned; his credibility with Torah-observant believers is essential for the ruling's acceptance.
14: Symeōn exēgēsato kathōs prōton ho theos epeskepsato labein ex ethnōn laon tō onomati autou ('Simeon described how God first visited to take from the Gentiles a people for his name'): James uses Symeōn — the Semitic/Aramaic form of Peter's name — signaling a Hebrew-language discourse register. Episkeptomai (visit, look after) is the OT divine-visit verb (Exod 4:31; Luke 1:68); God's Gentile-inclusion is an act of divine visitation, not human initiative.
15: toutō symphōnousin hoi logoi tōn prophētōn ('with this the words of the prophets agree'): James grounds the Cornelius-event interpretation in the prophetic scriptures — his hermeneutic is: divine act → scriptural confirmation. The prophets 'agree' (symphōnousin) with what has happened; the event is not a theological novelty but the realization of what Scripture anticipated. James's citation of Amos 9:11-12 LXX is the most important OT quotation in Acts after the Pentecost Joel-citation.
16: anorthōsō tēn skēnēn Dauid tēn peptōkuian ('I will rebuild the fallen tent of David'): James quotes Amos 9:11 LXX — the 'tent of David' (skēnē) is the fallen Davidic dynasty. The LXX differs significantly from the MT at vv.11-12; skēnē (tent/booth) reflects the LXX rendering of Hebrew sukkah. Anorthōsō (I will straighten up, rebuild) is the same word used for the lame man who stood straight (Luke 13:13); the Davidic restoration is a straightening-up of what had fallen.
17: hopōs an ekzētēsōsin hoi kataloipoi tōn anthrōpōn ton kyrion kai panta ta ethnē ('so that the remnant of humanity may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles'): the LXX of Amos 9:12 has 'humanity' (anthrōpōn) where the MT has 'Edom' — a reading difference that makes the prophecy explicitly universal. James's use of the LXX version is precise: he cites the form that supports the universal Gentile inclusion. Eph' hous epikeklētai to onoma mou ep' autous ('over whom my name is called') is the covenant-ownership formula applied to Gentile believers.
18: gnōsta ap' aiōnos ('known from of old'): the Gentile inclusion was not God's plan B but eternal intention now enacted. The statement is the capstone of James's scriptural argument: what is happening (Gentile conversion) is not a surprise development but the fulfillment of the divinely-foreknown plan. Gnōsta (known, manifest) — the same root as James's earlier 'God has made known' (egnōrisen) in his framing of v.14.
19: dio egō krinō mē parenochleisthai tois apo tōn ethnōn epistrephoysin epi ton theon ('therefore my judgment is not to trouble those from the Gentiles who are turning to God'): krinō (I judge, I decide) is James's formal ruling word — not a suggestion but a judicial determination. Parenochleō (trouble, harass, add burden) is strong: the Torah-requirement would constitute harassment of genuine converts. The ruling protects the Gentile believers from unnecessary burden while establishing minimal boundaries for table-fellowship.
20: all' episteilai autois tou apechesthai ... eidōlothuton kai porneia kai pniktou kai haimatos ('write to them to abstain from... food polluted by idols, sexual immorality, meat of strangled animals, blood'): the Apostolic Decree's four abstentions. The list draws on Lev 17-18, which applied certain prohibitions to the alien resident (ger) in Israel — the Gentile living within the covenant community. The Decree establishes table-fellowship conditions, not salvation conditions; it enables Gentile and Jewish believers to eat together.
21: Mōusēs gar ek geneōn archaīōn kata polin tous kērussontas auton echei ('Moses has had preachers in every city from ancient generations'): the reference to Moses being preached in every city gives the rationale for the Decree — Jewish communities throughout the diaspora already observe these practices, and Gentile believers who share table-fellowship with them need minimal common ground. The Decree is not soteriological but social-ecclesiological: enabling the mixed Jewish-Gentile church.
22: tote edoxe tois apostolois kai tois presbyterois syn holē tē ekklēsia ('then it seemed good to the apostles and elders with the whole church'): edoxe (seemed good, pleased) is the decision-consensus verb — the same root as v.28's edoxen tō pneumati tō hagiō kai hēmin. The council's decision is simultaneously the whole church's decision: apostolic, presbyteral, and congregational authority converge in the ruling. Eklexamenous andras ex autōn (choosing men from among themselves): Judas Barsabbas and Silas are sent as official delegates.
23: hoi apostoloi kai hoi presbyteroi adelphoi tois kata tēn Antiocheian kai Syrian kai Kilikian ('the apostles and elders, brothers, to the brothers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia'): the letter's address form is the standard Greek epistolary salutation adapted for Christian use. The senders identify as adelphoi (brothers) writing to adelphoi — the familial equality is built into the greeting. The geographic scope includes Cilicia (Paul's home region) — foreshadowing his later ministry there.
24: epeidie ēkousamen hoti tines ex hēmōn exelthontes etaraxan hymas logois ('we have heard that some went out from us and troubled you with words'): the council explicitly distances itself from the Judaizers — they went out 'from us' but without authorization (ois ou diesteilametha, 'to whom we gave no instructions'). The phrase anaskeuazontes tas psychas hymōn (unsettling your minds/souls) is the pastoral damage-assessment: not merely theological error but personal destabilization.
25: edoxen hēmin genomenois homothumadon ('it seemed good to us having become of one accord'): homothymadon appears here one final time — the apostolic unity that characterized Pentecost (2:1) now characterizes the council's decision. The unanimity is itself a sign; the Decree is not a compromise position but a genuinely unified judgment. Eklexamenois andras pempsai pros hymas: the choice of delegates rather than a letter alone shows the council understood that the decree needed personal exposition.
26: anthrōpois paradedōkosi tas psychas autōn hyper tou onomatos tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou ('men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ'): paradidōmi tēn psychēn (give up one's life) is the martyr-language; the council's endorsement of Paul and Barnabas includes an official recognition of their life-risking service. The phrase validates their authority to the Antioch church: these are men whose credentials are written in physical danger.
27: Ioudan kai Silan kai autous dia logou apangelountas ta auta ('Judas and Silas who themselves will tell you the same things by word of mouth'): the letter needs oral amplification — written decrees require living interpreters who can answer questions and provide the pastoral rationale. Silas (Sylvanus in Paul's letters, 1 Thess 1:1; 2 Cor 1:19; 1 Pet 5:12) becomes Paul's companion in Acts 15:40-18:5.
28: edoxen gar tō pneumati tō hagiō kai hēmin ('it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us'): the formula is the council's distinctive self-understanding — the ruling is not merely human consensus but Spirit-guided discernment. The Spirit who had been directing the mission (8:29; 10:44; 13:2; 15:8) is now co-author of the ruling. The phrase has become the template for Christian conciliar theology: church decisions can reflect Spirit-guidance through genuinely human deliberative processes.
29: apechesthai eidōlothutōn kai haimatos kai pniktōn kai porneias (abstain from idol-meat, blood, strangled animals, and sexual immorality): the four Apostolic Decree terms. The first three relate to food-purity (levitical requirements for resident aliens: Lev 17:8-16); porneia (sexual immorality) may include marriages within prohibited degrees or sexual practices associated with Gentile worship. The minimal list facilitates table-fellowship while protecting the community from idolatry-adjacent practices.
30: apelythēsan oun kateelthon eis Antiocheian kai synagagontes to plēthos epedōkan tēn epistolēn ('they were sent off and went to Antioch and gathering the congregation they delivered the letter'): the delegation's mission is the delivery and reception of the ruling within the whole community (plēthos, the entire congregation); the council decision becomes the church's shared possession through this communal reading. The formal assembly mirrors the synagogue's Torah-reading practice.
31: anagnontes de echarēsan epi tē paraklēsei ('when they read it they were glad for the encouragement'): chara (joy) again marks the arrival of the right resolution; the Antioch church's response to the letter is the joy of a burden removed. paraklēsei (encouragement, comfort): the letter is received not as a legal ruling but as paraklesis — the same Spirit-comfort that characterizes prophetic ministry throughout Acts.
32: Ioudas te kai Silas kai autoi prophētai ontes dia logou pollou parekalesan tous adelphous kai epestērixen ('Judas and Silas, being themselves prophets, with much speaking encouraged the brothers and strengthened them'): the prophetic gift (prophētai) is deployed in pastoral paraklēsis and epistērizō (strengthening, establishing). This is the OT prophetic role carried into the new covenant community — the prophet speaks for God's people's strengthening, not only for prediction.
33: apolytheisan met' eirēnēs apo tōn adelphōn pros tous aposteilantas autous ('they were sent off with peace from the brothers to those who had sent them'): the met' eirēnēs (with peace) farewell is the standard blessing-release for travelers and delegates; it carries the shalom-content of wholeness and right relationship. The mission's business is completed; the delegates return to their sending community while the Antioch church retains its own peace.
34: Verse 34 ('But Silas chose to remain there') is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts (p74, Sinaiticus) and is likely a scribal insertion to explain why Silas is available in Antioch for Paul's invitation in v.40. Without v.34, Luke's narrative has Silas departing with Judas (v.33) and being chosen by Paul from Antioch in v.40 — compatible if Silas returned to Antioch before Paul's invitation. The variant is text-critical, not theological.
35: Paulos de kai Barnabas dietribon en Antiocheia didaskontes kai euangelizomenoi meta kai heterōn pollōn ('Paul and Barnabas remained in Antioch teaching and preaching the word of the Lord along with many others'): the imperfect dietribon (were staying, remaining) marks the extended period of settled ministry. The plural heterōn pollōn (many others) shows the Antioch church had a substantial teaching community, not dependent on apostolic figures alone for its ongoing formation.
36: episkefsōmetha tous adelphous kata polin pasan en hais katēngelamen ton logon tou kyriou pōs echousin ('let us visit the brothers in every city where we proclaimed the word of the Lord, to see how they are'): Paul's pastoral instinct — the mission's goal is not proclamation only but the formation and ongoing health of communities. Pōs echousin (how they are faring) is the welfare-check language of family responsibility; Paul treats the planted churches as kin requiring continued attention.
37: Barnabas de ebouleto symparalabein kai ton Iōannēn ton kaloumenon Markon ('Barnabas wanted to take John called Mark with them'): the same Mark whose departure at Pamphylia (13:13) Paul regarded as desertion. Barnabas's advocacy for Mark is consistent with his character as huios paraklēseōs (son of encouragement, 4:36) — he had vouched for Paul (9:27) and now vouches for Mark. The conflict is not merely personal preference but a real missional disagreement about reliability under pressure.
38: Paulos de ēxiou ton apostanta ap' autōn apo Pamphylias kai mē synelthonta autois eis to ergon ('Paul thought it wrong to take with them one who had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia and had not continued with them in the work'): apostanta (having withdrawn, deserted) is Paul's characterization — aphistēmi is the apostasy verb. The issue is not the quality of Mark's future work but the precedent set by rewarding departure from a mission. Paul's standard is consistent: the ergon (the work) requires reliable commitment.
39: egeneto de paroxysmos hōste apochōristhēnai autous ap' allēlōn ('there was such a sharp disagreement that they parted company'): paroxysmos (sharp provocation, paroxysm) is a strong word — the medical term for a fever-crisis; in LXX it describes God's anger (Deut 29:28; Ps 106:32). Luke records the rupture without editorial verdict; the narrative outcome (two mission teams, doubled coverage) suggests God's providential use of the conflict, though this is implicit rather than stated.
40: Paulos de epilexamenos Silan exēlthen paradotheis tē chariti tou kyriou hypo tōn adelphōn ('Paul chose Silas and departed, being commended to the grace of the Lord by the brothers'): the parallel with the Antioch sending of 13:3 — community prayer and commendation — confirms Silas as the legitimate replacement partner. Tē chariti tou kyriou (to the grace of the Lord) frames the commission: the mission proceeds under divine grace-protection, not apostolic self-authorization.
41: diērcheto de tēn Syrian kai Kilikian epistērizōn tas ekklēsias ('he was going through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches'): the route through Syria and Cilicia revisits the communities formed in Paul's early post-conversion ministry (Gal 1:21); the epistērizōn (strengthening, establishing) verb matches 14:22 and 15:32 — consolidation is the constant companion of advance. The second missionary journey begins as continuation and consolidation before it becomes new expansion.
